text: The surgeon mobilizes the sigmoid colon, clearing the pelvis of bowel to allow the assistant to use the assistant port effectively during the laparoscopic myomectomy.

The surgeon identifies the uterine arteries and performs bilateral coagulation, causing the uterus to appear blanched. An incision is made on the most prominent fibroid to facilitate its removal. Multiple incisions are made over individual fibroids to promote better healing and reduce hematoma formation.

The surgeon uses a monopolar device to incise and remove the third fibroid from the posterior aspect of the uterus, applying firm traction and counter traction. The fourth fibroid is then incised and removed, with minimal blood loss observed despite the absence of vasopressin. The fibroids are large, each approximately five to seven centimeters. The final fibroid, located at the right anterofundal region of the uterus, is incised separately using the monopolar device.

The surgeon opens the uterine myometrium and removes the fibroid. Laparoscopic suturing begins using a routine one number vicryl suture, with box type stitches applied to ensure good depth and complete hemostasis. The suturing is performed through a suprapubic port, with deep bites taken on the anterior aspect to promote healing and prevent hematoma formation. Each uterine incision is sutured independently.
The surgeon uses a laparoscopic morcellator to remove the fibroids from the peritoneal cavity. The fibroids can be placed in an endo bag or morsef bag before extraction.
